name: MediaCup
short_description: An ordinary coffee cup invisibly augmented with sensing, processing, and infrared communication so it can broadcast its context into a networked environment.
long_description: MediaCup is an everyday coffee cup whose base houses a temperature sensor, a movement sensor, a microcontroller, and an ultra-low-power infrared transceiver, letting the object sense how it is being handled and report derived states such as drinking, stationary/filled, and hot or cold. It is a foundational ubiquitous-computing experiment in embedding context-awareness into a familiar artifact rather than a screen, and in using the resulting signals (for example the density of co-located cups) as ambient context for surrounding information systems. As a 'smart object' precedent it shows how mundane things can become communicating participants in an environment without changing how people use them.
ai_note: Context states are derived by threshold and rule-based interpretation of temperature and movement sensor signals; no machine learning runs in the system, so the 'some' hint is overridden to 'none'.

images.caption: MediaCup prototype: sensors, microcontroller, and infrared diode built into the base of an ordinary coffee cup.
images.alt_text: A white ceramic coffee cup whose base has been opened to reveal a small circuit board carrying sensors, a microcontroller, and an infrared transmitter.

sources.title: Mediacups: experience with design and use of computer-augmented everyday artefacts
The MediaCup: Awareness Technology Embedded in an Everyday Object

interactions.title: The cup tells the office you stepped out
interactions.description: Lift the cup, sip, and set it down and its base reads the motion and the warmth of the coffee, deriving states like 'being drunk from', 'filled and stationary', or 'cold and idle' and quietly broadcasting them over infrared to the room — no button, no screen, you just use the cup as a cup.

interactions.title: An electronic doorplate that says 'Meeting'
interactions.description: When several cups holding hot coffee are moving inside one room, the door's electronic plate flips to 'Meeting', so a passer-by can tell from the hallway that people have gathered without anyone announcing it.

interactions.title: Meetings detected by the density of warm cups
interactions.description: Because each cup reports where it is and whether its drink is hot, a cluster of co-located hot cups is read as a spontaneous get-together — context the surrounding systems infer rather than something a person logs.

interactions.title: Overhead infrared cells track where the cup is
interactions.description: Ceiling-mounted IrDA transceivers, each covering roughly a square metre, pick up a cup's beacon as it travels between offices, so the system knows which room the cup — and by extension its owner's coffee break — is currently in.

interactions.title: Living with a cup you recharge in its saucer
interactions.description: The team used the cups in daily office life as ordinary mugs, dropping them onto an inductive coaster/saucer to top up the battery between coffees — the augmentation stayed invisible and the ritual of getting coffee was unchanged.

interactions.title: Hearing a distant office as soft background noise
interactions.description: In an Ambient Telepresence study, the everyday activity captured by MediaCups and other instrumented objects in one office was rendered at a remote workplace as subtle ambient sound, giving colleagues a peripheral, non-intrusive sense that the other room was busy.

interactions.title: A coffee break that nudges your calendar status
interactions.description: Since a warm, in-motion cup already signals you are away from the desk and probably with others, that same state could flip a shared status to 'on a break' or hold notifications — grounded in the cup's existing drinking/stationary/hot states and IR location, no new sensing required.

interactions.title: The cup that learns your hydration rhythm
interactions.description: By logging the cadence of sip events and refills it already detects, a deskful of MediaCups could surface a gentle ambient cue when the afternoon coffee has gone cold and untouched for an hour — purely from the temperature and movement signals the base reports.
